english: a dog), rgyab-glág (rgyab glag) perh. different species of eagles.
often used erroneously instead of rlag-pa (rlag pa).

opportunity, occasion, possibility, glags _o_tšól-bá (glags 'tshol ba) to seek an occasion, to look for an opportunity; da glags rnyéd-par dug (da glags rnyed par dug) now the favourable point of time seems to have come Glr.; esp. opportunity of doing harm to another, of getting a hold on him; glags rnyéd-par mi gyur (glags rnyed par mi gyur), he will not be able to get at you, to do you harm; gsó-glags med (gso glags med) there is no possibility of helping him, he is incurable Med.; bzód-glags med (bzod glags med) intolerable, insupportable, frq.

(Bal. *glaṅ* (glang)) 1. ox, bullock. - 2. elephant. - 3 Taurus, the Bull, in the zodiac. Comp. glaṅ-glád (glang glad) 'bullock- or elephant-brains'; soap being made of such, acc. to popular belief: C. soap (Schr. gla-glad (gla glad)). - glaṅ-to (glang to) the Indian bison, Bos taurus indicus, Lh. - glaṅ-túg (glang thug), glaṅ óg-ċan (glang og can) a bull. - glaṅ-dár-ma (glang dar ma) n. of a king of Tibet, living about 1000 after Christ, notorious for his hostility against the hierarchy of the Lamas. - glaṅ-dór (glang dor) a team of bullocks. - glaṅ-sná (glang sna) the trunk or proboscis of an elephant; a plant so called on account of the long spiral spur of its corolla, Pedicularis Hookeriana. - glaṅ-po (glang po) = glaṅ (glang). - glaṅ-po-čé (glang po che), glaṅ-čéṅ (glang chen), elephant, glaṅ-mo (glang mo) a female elephant, glaṅ-ṗrúg (glang phrug) the young of an elephant. - glaṅ-bu (glang bu) a young bullock, glaṅ-rú (glang ru) a bullock's horn; also a large fork used by the Tibetan soldiers to rest the musket on, when firing (Hook. II., 235). - ṗa-glaṅ (pha glang) = glaṅ-túg (glang thug).
Med., gzer-glaṅ (gzer glang) W., colic, gripes, spasms in the stomach, and similar affections; glaṅ-šú (glang shu) Med.?
a large kind of alpine willow.

1. the head, glád-la (glad la) round the head, e.g. to brandish a sword, Glr.; as postposition used in a general sense: close over, čui glád-la (chu'i glad la) close over the water. - 2. brain Med., cf. klád-pa (klad pa).
to thin Sch. Cf. lhad (lhad), slád-pa (slad pa).

1. = glón-pa (glon pa), to patch, botch, mend; glan brgyáb-pa (glan brgyab pa) Sch., glán-par byė́d-pa (glan par byed pa) Lt. id.; lhán-pa glán-pa (lhan pa glan pa) to sew on a patch Lex. - 2. to return, lan (lan) an answer, to reply, rejoin Lex. - 3. C. col for glén-pa (glen pa); so also occasionally in books.

prop. island, but usually: continent, part of the globe, viz. one of the four imaginary parts of the earth, as taught by the geographers of Tibet, or rather of ancient India: lus-págs (lus pags) the part east of the Sumeru, of a semicircular shape; _o_dzaṃ-bu-gliṅ ('dzam bu gling) in the south, triangular; ba-glaṅ-spyód (ba glang spyod) in the west, circular; sgra-mi-snyán (sgra mi snyan) in the north, square. The general character of the first of these parts is described as being żi-ba (zhi ba) tranquil; that of the second as being rgyás-pa (rgyas pa) rich; that of the third as being dbaṅ-ldán (dbang ldan) strong, and that of the fourth as being drág-po (drag po) wild. In a more general sense: region, country, so Nepal is frq. denominated rin-po-čéi gliṅ (rin po che'i gling) the country of jewels and treasures, Urgyan mká-groi gliṅ (ur rgyan mkha' 'gro'i gling) the country of the Dakini, as is also Lahoul, in local chronicles; byai gliṅ (bya'i gling) region or country of birds Glr.; the word is also not unfrequently a component part of the names of towns and villages. - gliṅ-prán (gling phran) prop. a little island, generally one of the small continents, of which there are eight, acc. to the above mentioned geographical system; also island in general. - gliṅ-ka (gling ka) a small uncultivated river-island, or low-land C.

fife, flageolet, made of one piece of wood and much like those used in Europe as play-things for children; it is the common musical instrument of herdsmen, and often consists of two pipes; ṗred-gliṅ (phred gling) flute, piccolo-flute, mostly of metal; dge-gliṅ (dge gling) a larger musical
